name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
مَآ أَصَابَ مِن مُّصِيبَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِٱللَّهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (١١)
```

```english
No affliction visits [anyone] except by Allah’s leave. Whoever has faith in Allah, He guides his heart, and Allah has knowledge of all things. (11)
```

```arabic
وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَىٰ رَسُولِنَا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٢)
```

```english
Obey Allah and obey the Apostle; but if you turn away, then Our Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms. (12)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١٣)
```

```english
Allah—there is no god except Him—in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (13)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّ مِنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُمْ وَأَوْلَـٰدِكُمْ عَدُوًّا لَّكُمْ فَٱحْذَرُوهُمْ ۚ وَإِن تَعْفُوا۟ وَتَصْفَحُوا۟ وَتَغْفِرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٤)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Indeed, you have enemies among your spouses and children; so beware of them. Yet if you excuse, forbear, and forgive, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (14)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَآ أَمْوَٰلُكُمْ وَأَوْلَـٰدُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عِندَهُۥٓ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٥)
```

```english
Rather, your possessions, and children are a test, and Allah—with Him is a great reward! (15)
```

```arabic
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا ٱسْتَطَعْتُمْ وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ وَأَنفِقُوا۟ خَيْرًا لِّأَنفُسِكُمْ ۗ وَمَن يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِۦ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (١٦)
```

```english
So be wary of Allah, as much as you can, and listen and obey, and spend [in the way of Allah]; that is better for yourselves. Those who are saved from their own greed—it is they who are the felicitous. (16)
```

```arabic
إِن تُقْرِضُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا يُضَـٰعِفْهُ لَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ شَكُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (١٧)
```

```english
If you lend Allah a good loan, He shall multiply it for you and forgive you, and Allah is all-appreciative, all-forbearing, (17)
```

```arabic
عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١٨)
```

```english
Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (18)
```

## (Commentary)

This section begins the primary purpose of the Surah following the preceding introduction and preamble, which is to exhort the believers to spend in the way of Allah and to remain patient (الصبر) — *the steadfast endurance and restraint of the soul* — amidst the calamities that befall them during the course of striving (المجاهدة) in the way of Allah, the Glorified.

The mention of calamity and the allusion to patience were presented first so that the context would be cleared for the subsequent exhortation to spend, thereby cutting off any excuse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما أَصابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ وَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُ وَاللهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ** — *No affliction visits [anyone] except by Allah’s leave. Whoever has faith in Allah, He guides his heart, and Allah has knowledge of all things.* (64:11). Calamity (المصيبة) is a descriptive term whose usage has become common for adverse events accompanied by harm. Leave (الإذن) — *the granting of permission and the absence of hindrance* — necessitates knowledge of that which is permitted; it is not merely "knowledge" itself, as some have claimed.

From what has preceded, it becomes clear, firstly, that His leave, the Exalted, regarding the action of any cause (سبب) is the "letting be" (التخلية) between that cause and its effects (مسبباته) by removing the obstacles that intervene between them. Thus, He does not prevent the cause from acting upon its object according to its causal nature. For example, fire necessitates the burning of cotton unless there is a separation between them or moisture present. Removing the separation and the moisture from the cotton, while being cognizant of this, constitutes "leave" for the fire to act upon the cotton according to its essential property, namely, burning.

In general custom (العرف العام), the use of "leave" (الإذن) was restricted to cases where the one being permitted is a rational agent, because the concept of "notification" (الإعلام) is embedded in its meaning. Thus, it is said: "I gave leave to so-and-so to do such-and-half," but it is not said: "I gave leave to the fire to burn," or "I gave leave to the horse to gallop." However, the Noble Quran uses the term in a way that encompasses both rational and non-rational entities through conceptual analysis (التحليل), as in His saying: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** — *We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave* (4:64), and His saying: **وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَبَاتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ** — *As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord* (7:58). It is not improbable that this generalization is based on what the Quran conveys regarding the permeation of knowledge and perception (الإدراك) throughout all existents, as we previously discussed in the commentary on His saying: **قَالُوا أَنْطَقَنَا اللهُ الَّذِي أَنْطَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ** — *They will say, ‘Allah gave us speech, [He] who gives speech to all things’* (41:21).

In any case, no action is completed by any actor, nor does any effect proceed from any influencer, except by the leave of Allah, the Glorified. For causes that are incomplete and have obstacles which, if realized, would prevent their effect, His leave consists of His removing those obstacles. For causes that are complete and have no obstacle to hinder them, His leave consists of His not creating any obstacles for them. Thus, their effect is accompanied by His leave without any separation.

Secondly: Calamities (المصائب) — which are the events that strike a human being and produce adverse, disliked effects — only occur by the leave of Allah, the Glorified, just as good deeds (الحسنات) do, because His leave encompasses the issuance of every effect from every influencer.

Thirdly: This leave is an ontological leave (إذن تكويني) — *the existential permission inherent in the laws of creation* — which is distinct from the legislative leave (إذن تشريعي) — *the legal permission that removes a prohibition from an act*. Thus, the occurrence of a calamity is accompanied by an ontological leave from Allah for its happening, even if it involves a forbidden injustice (ظلم). The fact that injustice is forbidden and not permitted pertains to the realm of legislation (التشريع), not the realm of ontology (التكوين).

For this reason, some calamities are such that patience (الصبر) regarding them is not permissible, nor is enduring them permitted; rather, a human being must resist them as much as possible, such as injustices (المظالم) related to honor and life.

From this, it becomes clear that the calamities at which patience is exhorted are those where the afflicted person is not commanded to repel or refrain from enduring them, such as general cosmic calamities like death and illness, in which human choice (الاختيار) has no role. As for those in which choice has a role, such as injustices that are somewhat related to choice — like those directed at honor — a human being should guard against them as much as possible.

His saying: **وَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُ** — *Whoever has faith in Allah, He guides his heart*. The apparent context of His saying: **ما أَصابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** implies that Allah, the Glorified, possesses knowledge (علم) and volition (مشية) regarding the events that distress a human being. No calamity strikes him except after His knowledge and volition. Thus, no cosmic cause is independent in its effect; rather, it is the system of creation (نظام الخلقة), which has no Lord (رب) possessing it except its Creator. No event occurs and no incident takes place except by His knowledge and volition. Therefore, what struck him was never meant to miss him, and what missed him was never meant to strike him.

This is the same reality explained in a different manner in His saying: **مَا أَصَابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَبْرَأَهَا إِنَّ ذَلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ** — *No affliction visits the earth or your selves but it is in a Book before We bring it about—that is indeed easy for Allah* (57:22).

Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين), and His general Lordship (الربوبية) — *His absolute ownership, governance, and sustenance of all creation* — necessitates that He alone owns everything; there is no true owner (مالك) besides Him. The system running through existence is the sum total of His modes of disposal (تصرفات) over His creation. No mover moves and no still thing rests except by His leave. No actor acts and no recipient receives except by His prior knowledge and a volition that His knowledge and volition never fail, nor is His decree (قضاؤه) ever repelled.

Thus, the conviction (الإذعان) that He, the Exalted, is Allah, results in the soul being guided to these realities, and the heart (القلب) finding tranquility (اطمئنان) and stillness, free from agitation and anxiety caused by attachment to apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) and the attribution of bitter calamities and misfortunes to them instead of to Allah, the Glorified.

This is the meaning of His saying: **وَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِاللهِ يَهْدِ قَلْبَهُ** — *Whoever has faith in Allah, He guides his heart*.

It has been said that the meaning of the sentence is: "Whoever believes in the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah and is patient with the command of Allah, He guides his heart to the *istirja'* (الاسترجاع) — *the act of returning to Allah by saying 'To Allah we belong and to Him we shall return'* — until he says: **إِنَّا للهِ وَإِنَّا إِلَيْهِ رَاجِعُونَ**." In this view, patience is included within the meaning of faith (الإيمان).

It has also been said the meaning is: "Whoever believes in Allah, He guides his heart to what he ought to do; if he is tested, he is patient; if he is given, he is grateful; and if he is wronged, he forgives." This view is close to what we have presented.

His saying: **وَاللهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ** — *and Allah has knowledge of all things* — is an emphasis of the preceding exception. It may also be an allusion to what is conveyed by His saying: **مَا أَصَابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِي كِتَابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَبْرَأَهَا** (57:22).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ فَإِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَإِنَّما عَلى رَسُولِنَا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ** — *Obey Allah and obey the Apostle; but if you turn away, then Our Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms.* (64:12). The apparent repetition of "Obey" (أطيعوا), rather than saying "Obey Allah and the Apostle," indicates a difference in what is intended by the obedience. Obedience to Allah, the Exalted, refers to submission (الانقياد) to Him in the religious laws (شرائع الدين) He has legislated for them. Obedience to the Apostle refers to submission to him and compliance with what he commands by virtue of his authority (ولاية) over the nation (الأمة), as established for him by Allah.

His saying: **فَإِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَإِنَّما عَلى رَسُولِنَا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ** — *but if you turn away, then Our Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms*. Turning away (التولي) means averting oneself (الإعراض), and communication (البلاغ) means the act of conveying (التبليغ). The meaning is: If you turn away from obeying Allah in what He has legislated of the religion, or from obeying the Apostle in what he commands you as your leader (ولي أمركم), then Our Apostle will not compel you to obey, for he was not commanded to do so. He was only commanded to convey the message, and he has indeed conveyed it.

From this, it becomes clear that the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) command regarding matters beyond the established rulings and laws — namely, the conveying of Allah's message — is such that his command and prohibition in his capacity as a leader are Allah's command and prohibition. Obedience to him in both is part of the obedience to Allah, as indicated by the general nature of His saying: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطَاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ** (4:64). This verse suggests that obedience to the Apostle in whatever he commands or prohibits is absolutely permitted by Allah's leave. His leave for obedience to him necessitates His knowledge and volition for that obedience. Willing the obedience to a command or prohibition is willing the command or prohibition itself. Thus, the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) command and prohibition are Allah's command and prohibition, even if they concern matters beyond the laws and statutes established by Him, the Exalted.

Because the obedience to the Apostle returns to the obedience to Allah, there is a shift from the third person to the first person plural in His saying: **رَسُولِنَا** (Our Apostle), which also carries a subtle tone of threat.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ** — *Allah—there is no god but He—and in Allah let the faithful put their trust.* (64:13). This serves as the rationale (التعليل) for the obligation of obeying Allah, based on what was previously mentioned that obedience to the Apostle is part of obedience to Allah. To clarify: obedience in the sense of submission, following commands, and refraining from prohibitions is one of the aspects of servitude (العبودية). The effect of a master's ownership of his slave's person is his ownership of the slave's will and action; thus, the slave wills only what the master wants him to will, and acts only as the master wants him to act. Therefore, obedience is a form of servitude, as alluded to in His saying: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يَا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لَا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطَانَ** — *Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, “Do not worship Satan”* (36:60), where He rebukes them for "worshipping" Satan when they had merely obeyed him.

Thus, the obedience of the one who obeys toward the one obeyed is a type of worship (عبادة) of him. Since there is no object of worship (معبود) except Allah, there is no obedience except to Allah, exalted is His Name, or to those whose obedience He has commanded. The meaning is: Obey Allah, the Glorified, since there is no obedience except to an object of worship, and there is no true object of worship except Allah. Therefore, you must worship Him and not associate anything with Him by obeying and worshipping others, such as Satan or carnal desire (هوى النفس). This is the meaning of the sentence being in the position of providing the rationale.

From what has passed, the reason for specifying the attribute of Divinity (الألوهية) — which conveys the meaning of being the object of worship — rather than the attribute of Lordship (الربوبية) becomes clear; thus, He did not say: "Allah, there is no Lord (رب) besides Him."

His saying: **وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ** — *and in Allah let the faithful put their trust* — is an emphasis of the meaning of the preceding sentence, namely: **اللهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ**.

To clarify: Trust (التوكيل) is for a person to appoint another in his place to manage his affairs. This necessitates that the appointee's will stands in place of the trustor's will, and his action in place of the trustor's action. This corresponds in a way to obedience (الإطاعة), for the one who obeys makes his will and action subordinate to the will of the one obeyed. Thus, the will of the one obeyed stands in place of his own will, and his action becomes an object of the obeyed one's will, proceeding from it conceptually. Thus, obedience returns to being a form of trust in one respect, just as trust is a form of obedience in another.

The slave's obedience to his Lord is the subordination of his will to his Lord's will and performing the action in this manner. In other words, it is the preference of His will and the associated action over his own will and its associated action.

Therefore, obeying Him, the Exalted, in what He has legislated for His servants and what is related to it, is a type of reliance (التوكل) — *the act of placing one's trust and affairs in the hands of Allah* — upon Him. Obedience to Him is obligatory for whoever knows Him and believes in Him; thus, "in Allah let the faithful put their trust" and Him let them obey. As for those who do not know Him or believe in Him, no true obedience can be realized from them.

It has become clear from the preceding that faith and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) are a type of reliance upon Allah, the Exalted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِنَّ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ وَأَوْلادِكُمْ عَدُوًّا لَكُمْ فَاحْذَرُوهُمْ** — *O you who have faith! Indeed you have enemies among your wives and children; so beware of them.* (64:14).

The word "among" (مِنْ) in "among your wives" (مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ) denotes partitivity (التبعيض). The context of the address using the phrase "O you who have faith" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) and linking the enmity to them implies a rationale: that they are enemies to them *insofar as they are believers*. Enmity on account of faith is only realized through their efforts to turn the believers away from the foundation of faith or from righteous deeds, such as spending in the way of Allah and migrating (الهجرة) from the land of disbelief, or by inducing them toward disbelief or destructive sins (المعاصي الموبقة), such as stinginess in spending in the way of Allah out of compassion for children and spouses, or through usurpation and acquiring wealth through unlawful means.

Thus, Allah, the Glorified, considers some children and spouses as enemies to the believers in their faith when they lead them to abandon faith in Allah, or to abandon some righteous deeds, or to commit some destructive major sins. Sometimes the believers obey them in some of these matters out of compassion and love for them; therefore, Allah commanded them to beware of them.

His saying: **وَإِنْ تَعْفُوا وَتَصْفَحُوا وَتَغْفِرُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — *But if you excuse, forbear, and forgive, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful.* (64:14). Al-Raghib said: Pardon (العفو) is the intent to take something... until he said: "I pardoned him" means I intended the removal of his sin, turning away from it. He said: Forbearance (الصفح) is the abandonment of reprimand, and it is more profound than pardon; for this reason, the Exalted said: **فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ اللهُ بِأَمْرِهِ** — *So pardon and forbear until Allah ordains His edict* (2:109). A person may pardon but not forbear. He said: Forgiveness (الغفر) is the covering that protects from defilement... and *al-ghufran* and *al-maghfirah* from Allah is His protecting the servant from being touched by punishment. He said: **غُفْرانَكَ رَبَّنا** — *[We crave] Your forgiveness, our Lord* (2:285), and **مَغْفِرَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ** — *forgiveness from your Lord* (57:21), and **وَمَنْ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ إِلَّا اللهُ** — *and who forgives sins except Allah?* (3:135). [End of quote].

Thus, in His saying: "excuse, forbear, and forgive," there is an exhortation toward complete overlooking of the children and spouses if any signs of the aforementioned enmity appear from them — while remaining cautious of being led into trial (الافتتان) by them.

In His saying: **فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — *then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful* — if the intended meaning is specifically His forgiveness and mercy for the addressees should they excuse, forbear, and forgive, then it is a beautiful promise to them in return for their righteous deed, as in His saying: **وَلْيَعْفُوا وَلْيَصْفَحُوا أَلَا تُحِبُّونَ أَنْ يَغْفِرَ اللهُ لَكُمْ** — *Let them excuse and forbear. Do you not love that Allah should forgive you?* (24:22).

If the intended meaning is His general forgiveness and mercy without being restricted to the context of the address, it conveys that forgiveness and mercy are among the attributes of Allah, the Glorified. Thus, if they excuse, forbear, and forgive, they have characterized themselves with the attributes of Allah and adopted His ethics.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ وَأَوْلادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ وَاللهُ عِنْدَهُ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ** — *Your possessions and your children are only a trial, and Allah—with Him is a great reward.* (64:15). Trial (الفتنة) — *that by which one is tested and examined* — and the fact that wealth and children are a trial is because they are the "adornment of life" (زينة الحياة) to which the soul is attracted. Thus, one is tried and distracted by them from the important matters of the Hereafter and obedience to the Lord. The Exalted said: **الْمَالُ وَالْبَنُونَ زِينَةُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا** — *Wealth and children are an adornment of the life of the world* (18:46).

The sentence is an allusion (كناية) to the prohibition of being distracted by them and neglecting one's duty toward Allah by inclining toward them. This is emphasized by His saying: **وَاللهُ عِنْدَهُ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ** — *and Allah—with Him is a great reward*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ** — *So be God-conscious as much as you can* (64:16), meaning to the extent of your capability — as indicated by the context, which is one of invitation and exhortation to listen, obey, spend, and strive in the way of Allah. The sentence is a derivation (تفريع) from His saying: **إِنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ** etc. The meaning is: Be God-conscious of Him to the extent of your capability and do not leave any part of godwariness (الاتقاء) that is within your power and effort. Thus, the verse follows the same course as His saying: **اتَّقُوا اللهَ حَقَّ تُقاتِهِ** — *be God-conscious of Allah with the consciousness that is His due* (3:102). The verse is not concerned with negating the obligation of godwariness beyond one's capability and power, as in His saying: **وَلَا تُحَمِّلْنَا مَا لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا بِهِ** — *and do not lay upon us what we have no strength to bear* (2:286).

From what has passed, it has become clear:

First: There is no contradiction between the two verses, namely His saying: **فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ** and His saying: **اتَّقُوا اللهَ حَقَّ تُقاتِهِ**. The difference between them is like the difference in quantity and quality. His saying: **فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ** is a command to encompass all instances within one's capability with godwariness. His saying: **اتَّقُوا اللهَ حَقَّ تُقاتِهِ** is a command to embody, in each instance of godwariness, the *true* godwariness rather than its mere shadow or form.

Second: The invalidity of the claim made by some that His saying: **فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ** abrogates (ناسخ) His saying: **اتَّقُوا اللهَ حَقَّ تُقاتِهِ**, which is evident.

His saying: **وَاسْمَعُوا وَأَطِيعُوا وَأَنْفِقُوا خَيْراً لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ** — *and listen and obey, and spend for your own good* (64:16), is a clarification and emphasis of His saying: **فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ**. Listening (السمع) is response and acceptance, which pertains to the level of internal commitment. Obedience (الطاعة) is submission, which pertains to the level of action. The spending (الإنفاق) intended here is the giving of wealth in the way of Allah.

The phrase "for your own good" (خَيْراً لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ) is in the accusative case due to a deleted verb — according to *al-Kashshaf* — and the estimation is "believe for your own good." It is also possible that "spend" (أَنْفِقُوا) implies the meaning of "send forth" or something similar, given the context. In His saying: "for your own selves" (لِأَنْفُسِكُمْ), rather than saying "for your own good" (خيرا لكم), there is an added reassurance to their souls, meaning that the spending is good for you and none but yourselves will benefit from it, as it leads to the expansion of your means and the increase of your power to meet the needs of your society.

His saying: **وَمَنْ يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ** — *And those who are saved from their own greed—it is they who are the felicitous.* (64:16). The commentary on this has already been provided in the exegesis of Surah al-Hashr.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنْ تُقْرِضُوا اللهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً يُضاعِفْهُ لَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ وَاللهُ شَكُورٌ حَلِيمٌ** — *If you lend Allah a good loan, He will multiply it for you and forgive you, and Allah is all-appreciative, all-forbearing.* (64:17). What is intended by "lending to Allah" is spending in His way. Allah called it "lending to Allah" and called the spent wealth a "good loan" (قرضاً حسناً) to urge and encourage them toward it.

His saying: **يُضاعِفْهُ لَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ** — *He will multiply it for you and forgive you* — is an allusion to the excellence of His reward in this world and the Hereafter.

The All-Appreciative (الشكور), the All-Forbearing (الحليم), the Knower of the Unseen and the Manifest (عالم الغيب والشهادة), the All-Mighty (العزيز), and the All-Wise (الحكيم) are five of the Beautiful Names of Allah (أسماء الله الحسنى) whose explanations have preceded. The reason for their suitability to what was commanded in the verse — listening, obedience, and spending — is evident.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ وَأَوْلادِكُمْ عَدُوًّا لَكُمْ فَاحْذَرُوهُمْ** — "This was because when a man intended to migrate (الهجرة), his son and wife would cling to him and say: 'We adjure you by Allah not to leave us, for we will be lost after you.' Some of them would obey their families and stay behind; so Allah warned them against their children and wives and forbade them from obeying them. Others would proceed and leave them, saying: 'By Allah, if you do not migrate with me — and then Allah joins me and you together in the land of migration — I will never benefit you with anything.'

When Allah joined him and them together, Allah commanded him to be cautious through good relations, so He said: **وَإِنْ تَعْفُوا وَتَصْفَحُوا وَتَغْفِرُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ**."

**I say:** This meaning is also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from several compilers of traditions on the authority of Ibn Abbas.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ وَأَوْلادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ**, narrated from Ibn Mardawayh from 'Ubadah ibn al-Samit and Abdullah ibn Abi Awfa from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): **لكل أمة فتنة وفتنة أمتي المال** — "For every nation there is a trial, and the trial of my nation is wealth."

**I say:** A similar narration is also reported from him on the authority of Ka'b ibn 'Iyad from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ahmad, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah, al-Hakim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Buraydah who said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was delivering a sermon when al-Hasan and al-Husayn (peace be upon them) approached, wearing two red shirts, walking and stumbling. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) descended from the pulpit, carried one on this side and one on that side, then ascended the pulpit and said: 'Allah spoke the truth when He said: **إِنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ وَأَوْلادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ**. When I looked at these two boys walking and stumbling, I could not remain patient until I cut off my speech and descended to them.'"

**I say:** This narration is not free from issues. How could trial (الفتنة) affect the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) when he is the master of the sincere prophets, infallible (معصوم), and aided by the Holy Spirit (روح القدس)?

Even more egregious in its tone than this tradition is what was narrated from Ibn Mardawayh from Abdullah ibn Umar: "While the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was delivering a sermon to the people on the pulpit, al-Husayn ibn Ali came out and tripped on a garment he was wearing. He fell and cried, so the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) descended from the pulpit.

When the people saw this, they rushed to al-Husayn, passing him from one to another until he reached the hands of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). He said: 'May Allah fight Satan! Indeed, the child is a trial. By Him in whose hand is my soul, I did not realize that I had descended from my pulpit.'"

Similar to this is what is narrated from Ibn al-Mundhir from Yahya ibn Abi Kathir who said: "The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) heard the crying of Hasan or Husayn, so the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The child is a trial; I went to him and I was not in my right mind (ما أعقل).'"

The proper course is to reject these narrations unless they are interpreted figuratively.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Ibn Shahrashub from *Tafsir Waki'*, narrated from Sufyan ibn Murrah al-Hamdani from 'Abd Khayr: "I asked Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) about His saying, the Exalted: **اتَّقُوا اللهَ حَقَّ تُقاتِهِ**. He said: 'By Allah, none acted upon it except the People of the Household (أهل بيت) of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). We remember Allah and do not forget Him; we thank Him and do not disbelieve in Him; and we obey Him and do not disobey Him.'

When this was revealed, the companions said: 'We cannot bear that.' So Allah revealed: **فَاتَّقُوا اللهَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُمْ**." [The tradition continues].

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, narrated from my father from al-Fadl ibn Abi Murrah who said: "I saw Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) performing circumambulation (يطوف) from the beginning of the night until the morning, saying: **اللهم وقني شح نفسي** — 'O Allah, save me from the greed of my soul.' I said: 'May I be your ransom, I have not seen you supplicate with anything other than this supplication.' He replied: 'And what is more severe than the greed of the soul? Indeed, Allah says: **وَمَنْ يُوقَ شُحَّ نَفْسِهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ**.'"
